title: practical english usage - Part 154
content:
He probably thinks you don't like him.

Examples: probably, certainly, definitely, clearly, obviously 2 adverbials        of  certainty:      probably,          certainly,         etc For rarely, seldom, never, hardly and scarcely in front position before verb + subject (‘ inversion’ ), ►    270.7.

Never ask her about her marriage.

Always look in the mirror before starting to drive.

However, always and never can begin imperative clauses.

I always/never get up early, (     Always/Never I get up early.) Always, ever, rarely, seldom and never cannot normally go in front position.

I see her occasionally.

Sometimes I think I'd like to live somewhere else.

Other positions are possible for most of them.

She must sometimes have wanted to run away.

We have never been invited to one of their parties.

My boss is often bad-tempered. I'm seldom late for work.

Have you ever played American football?

You can always come and stay with us if you want to.

It sometimes gets very windy here.   I have never seen a whale.

We usually go to Scotland in August.

always, rarely, ever, hardly ever, seldom, never Examples: usually, normally, often, frequently, sometimes, occasionally, 1  adverbials        of  indefinite       frequency:       usually,        often,      etc and after an auxiliary verb - for more exact details, ►    200).

Adverbials in this group most often go in mid-position (before a one-part verb co  mpletenes       s ____ ______ 198  indefinite       frequency,       certainty       and Section16 Adverbs     and   Adverbials grammar •  199   focusing adverbials She would never have been promoted if she hadn't changed Jobs.

You have definitely been working too hard.

When  there are two or more auxiliaries, the adverbial usually goes after the first.

It is probably too late now. I am obviously not welcome here.

She was always kind to me. (                 She always was kind-to-me?) c   after amlarelislwaslwere The train will probably be late.  You can almost see the sea from here.

He was definitely trying to get into the house.

She has never written to me. (                 She never has written to me.) b  after auxiliary verbs It certainly looks like rain. We nearly won the match.

I always play tennis on Saturdays, (     J play always tennis...) a  before one-word verbs the auxiliary. And they go after am/are/is/was/were.

verb has more than one part (e.g. has written, was trying), they normally go after Mid-position adverbials usually go before one-word verbs (e.g. play, won). If a 1 What    exactly      is  mid-position?

200mid-position:       details Only you could do a thing like that.  I feel really tired.

the words they modify. For details, see the entries on each adverbial.

Some of these adverbials can also go in other places in a clause, directly before The people at the meeting were mainly scientists.

She's my teacher, but she's also my friend.

We're only going for two days.

He's been everywhere - he's even been to Antarctica.

She neither said 'Thank you' nor looked at me.

Your bicycle just needs some oil - that's alL They  often go in mid-position.

Examples: also, just, even, only, mainly, mostly, either, or, neither, nor These adverbials 'point to’  one part of a clause.

199 focusing      adverbials not I visit my old school from time to time, (     I from time to time visit...) I sometimes visit my old school.

Longer adverbials do not usually go in mid-position. Compare: 4 longer     adverbials:       from      time     to   time,     etc grammar •  200  mid-position: details ultimately responsible...) '... he ultimately was responsible for the treatment...' (BrE ... he was Party has often criticised...) '.. . the Labor Party often has criticized police actions.' (BrE ... the Labour lead...) '... but it probably will lead to a vote../ (BrE ... but it will probably long been known ...) 'Britain long has been known as a land of law and order.' (BrE Britain has equivalents are given in brackets.

an American newspaper article on crime in Britain. The  most normal British As an extreme example, here are four sentences in a journalistic style taken from He probably has arrived by now. (also normal in AmE;  emphatic in BrE) He has probably arrived by now. (normal in both AmE   and BrE) especially in an informal style. Compare: auxiliary verbs and am/are/is/was/were, even when the verb is not emphasised, In American English (►   319), mid-position adverbials are often put before 4 mid-position         (details):    American      English I really AM  sorry.
